By default, virtual ports come from the `42000-43000` band. The allocator probes each port with `TcpListener::bind` to skip anything currently in use, and consults the persisted `ssg_virtual_ports` table to avoid reusing a number already allocated to another `(project, service)`.

You can override the band via env vars on the daemon process:

```bash
COAST_VIRTUAL_PORT_BAND_START=42000
COAST_VIRTUAL_PORT_BAND_END=43000
```

Set them when launching `coastd` to widen, narrow, or move the band. Changes only affect newly-allocated ports; persisted allocations are preserved.

When the band is exhausted, `coast ssg run` errors out with a clear message and a hint to widen the band or remove unused projects (`coast ssg rm --with-data` clears a project's allocations).

sections.heading: Persistence and lifecycle
sections.content:
## Persistence and lifecycle

Virtual-port rows survive normal lifecycle churn:

| Event | `ssg_virtual_ports` |
|---|---|
| `coast ssg build` (rebuild) | preserved |
| `coast ssg stop` / `start` / `restart` | preserved |
| `coast ssg rm` | preserved |
| `coast ssg rm --with-data` | dropped (per-project) |
| Daemon restart | preserved (rows are durable; reconciler re-spawns host socats on startup) |

The reconciler (`host_socat::reconcile_all`) runs once at daemon start and respawns any host socat that should be alive -- one per `(project, service, container_port)` for every SSG that's currently `running`.

sections.heading: Remote consumers
sections.content:
## Remote consumers

A remote Coast (created by `coast assign --remote ...`) reaches the local SSG through a reverse SSH tunnel. Both sides of the tunnel use the **virtual** port:

```
remote VM                              local host
+--------------------------+           +-----------------------------+
| consumer DinD            |           | daemon host socat           |
|  +--------------------+  |           |  LISTEN:   0.0.0.0:42000    |
|  | in-DinD socat      |  |           |  UPSTREAM: 127.0.0.1:54201  |
|  | LISTEN: alias:5432 |  |           +-----------------------------+
|  | -> hgw:42000       |  |                       ^
|  +--------------------+  |                       | (daemon socat)
|                          |                       |
|  ssh -N -R 42000:localhost:42000  <------------- |
+--------------------------+
```

- The local daemon spawns `ssh -N -R <virtual_port>:localhost:<virtual_port>` against the remote machine.
- The remote sshd needs `GatewayPorts clientspecified` so the bound port accepts traffic from the docker bridge (not just remote loopback).
- Inside the remote DinD, `extra_hosts: postgres: host-gateway` resolves `postgres` to the remote's host-gateway IP. The in-DinD socat forwards to `host-gateway:<virtual_port>`, which the SSH tunnel carries back to the local host's same `<virtual_port>` -- where the daemon's host socat continues the chain to the SSG.

Tunnels are coalesced per `(project, remote_host, service, container_port)` in the `ssg_shared_tunnels` table. Multiple consumer instances of the same project on one remote share **one** `ssh -R` process. The first-arriving instance spawns it; subsequent instances reuse it; the last-departing instance tears it down.

Because rebuilds change the dynamic port but never the virtual port, **rebuilding the SSG locally never invalidates the remote tunnel**. The local host socat updates its upstream, and the remote keeps connecting to the same virtual-port number.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.content:
## See Also

- [Consuming](CONSUMING.md) -- the consumer-side `from_group = true` wiring and `extra_hosts` setup
- [Checkout](CHECKOUT.md) -- canonical-port host bindings; the checkout socat targets the same virtual port
- [Lifecycle](LIFECYCLE.md) -- when virtual ports are allocated, when host socats spawn, when they get refreshed
- [Concept: Ports](../concepts_and_terminology/PORTS.md) -- canonical vs dynamic ports across the rest of Coast
- [Remote Coasts](../remote_coasts/README.md) -- the broader remote-machine setup that the SSH tunnels above slot into

sections.heading: SSG-Native Secrets
sections.content:
# SSG-Native Secrets

`Coastfile.shared_service_groups` accepts `[secrets.<name>]` blocks alongside `[shared_services.<name>]`. SSG secrets are extracted at `coast ssg build` time, stored encrypted in the daemon keystore under a per-project namespace, and injected into the SSG's inner services at `coast ssg run` time via a generated `compose.override.yml`.

This page documents the SSG-side pipeline. For consumer-side `[secrets.*]` (the credentials your app uses to talk to a shared service), see [Coastfile: Secrets](../coastfiles/SECRETS.md). The two layers are independent -- you can use either, both, or neither. See [Composition](#composition-with-consumer-side-secrets) below for how they interact.

sections.heading: Schema
sections.content:
## Schema

The `[secrets.<name>]` shape mirrors the regular Coastfile (same extractors, same `inject` syntax). See [Coastfile: Shared Service Groups -> [secrets.<name>]](../coastfiles/SHARED_SERVICE_GROUPS.md#secretsname) for the full reference.

```toml
[shared_services.postgres]
image = "postgres:16-alpine"
ports = [5432]
env = { POSTGRES_USER = "postgres" }

[secrets.pg_password]
extractor = "env"
var = "MY_PG_PASSWORD"
inject = "env:POSTGRES_PASSWORD"

[secrets.jwt]
extractor = "env"
var = "MY_JWT_VALUE"
inject = "file:/run/secrets/jwt"
```

By default, a secret is injected into every declared service. Add `services = [...]` to scope it:

```toml
[secrets.pg_password]
extractor = "env"
var = "MY_PG_PASSWORD"
inject = "env:POSTGRES_PASSWORD"
services = ["postgres"]
```

sections.heading: Build Pipeline
sections.content:
## Build Pipeline

`coast ssg build` runs an `Extracting secrets` step between compose synthesis and image pulls. Skipped silently when the Coastfile has no `[secrets]` block. Per declared secret:

1. The extractor registry runs the chosen extractor (`env` / `file` / `command` / `keychain` / 1password / custom `coast-extractor-<name>`).
2. The decrypted value lands in the keystore under `coast_image = "ssg:<project>"`. The `ssg:` prefix is a sentinel namespace that can never collide with a regular Coast keystore entry (project names can't contain `:`).
3. A `BuildProgressEvent::item` event streams out per secret so the SPA renders one row per extractor.
4. Existing `ssg:<project>` rows are deleted before the new extraction starts. **Rebuild semantics:** every `coast ssg build` re-extracts, and the keystore always reflects the most recent build's secret config.

The `manifest.json` carries a `secret_injects` array describing the *shape* (name, inject type, target, services) -- never the values. The build hash also folds in `secrets[].extractor`, `secrets[].inject`, and the per-extractor params, so a Coastfile edit that only changes secret config still produces a fresh `build_id`.

sections.heading: Run Pipeline
sections.content:
## Run Pipeline

`coast ssg run` runs a `Materializing secrets` step (step 6 of 7) between `Loading cached images` and `Starting inner services`. Skipped silently when `manifest.secret_injects` is empty.

The materialize step:

1. Opens the keystore and reads every row for `ssg:<project>`.
2. For each `manifest.secret_injects` entry, looks up the matching keystore row by name.
3. **`env:`** injects -> appends `{NAME: <decrypted>}` under `services.<svc>.environment` per service in the override.
4. **`file:`** injects -> writes decrypted bytes to `~/.coast/ssg/runs/<project>/secrets/<basename>` with mode `0600`, then appends a read-only volume entry under `services.<svc>.volumes`.
5. Renders a deterministic YAML override:

   ```yaml
   # Generated by coast-ssg at run time. Do not hand-edit.
   services:
     postgres:
       environment:
         POSTGRES_PASSWORD: "<decrypted>"
       volumes:
         - "/coast-runtime/secrets/jwt:/run/secrets/jwt:ro"
   ```

6. Writes the override to `~/.coast/ssg/runs/<project>/compose.override.yml`.

The per-run scratch dir is bind-mounted into the outer DinD at `/coast-runtime/`, alongside the immutable `/coast-artifact/`. The compose argv inside the SSG becomes:

```
docker compose -f /coast-artifact/compose.yml \
               -f /coast-runtime/compose.override.yml \
               -p <project>-ssg up -d --remove-orphans
```

`coast ssg start` re-runs `materialize_secrets`, so the override file is refreshed on every start. A `coast ssg secrets clear` between stop and start takes effect on the next start.

The artifact dir (`/coast-artifact/compose.yml`, manifest, ssg-coastfile) stays read-only and byte-stable across runs -- secrets never touch it. The override layer is the only mutable piece.

sections.heading: File-secret layout
sections.content:
### File-secret layout

```text
~/.coast/ssg/runs/<project>/
  compose.override.yml             # generated each run
  secrets/
    jwt                            # mode 0600, decrypted bytes
    tls_cert                       # mode 0600
```

Inside the SSG's outer DinD, the same directory is visible at `/coast-runtime/`. The compose override mounts each file into the inner service's container at the configured `inject_target`, read-only.

sections.heading: Lifecycle: keystore is never auto-purged
sections.content:
## Lifecycle: keystore is never auto-purged

The keystore is deliberately preserved across container teardown:

| Verb | Containers | Inner volumes | Keystore (`ssg:<project>`) |
|---|---|---|---|
| `coast ssg rm` | removed | preserved | **preserved** |
| `coast ssg rm --with-data` | removed | dropped | **preserved** |
| `coast ssg secrets clear` | unchanged | unchanged | **dropped** |

The reasoning: removing an SSG is about wiping containers and data. Destroying credentials a user laboriously extracted from a keychain or 1Password is a separate, more dangerous operation that should be explicit.

`coast ssg secrets clear` is idempotent. Running it twice in a row reports `Cleared 2`, then `Cleared 0`.

sections.heading: Doctor
sections.content:
## Doctor

`coast ssg doctor` includes one finding per declared secret:

- **`ok`** -- the manifest declares a secret AND the keystore has a matching row.
- **`info`** -- "Declared in Coastfile but missing from the keystore. Run `coast ssg build` to re-extract or remove the [secrets.<name>] block." Triggered when a manifest declaration lacks a keystore row -- usually because someone ran `coast ssg secrets clear` after the build, or copied an artifact between machines.

These findings are non-fatal; the SSG can run without `info` secrets, but the corresponding env vars / files won't be injected.

sections.heading: Composition with consumer-side `[secrets.*]`
sections.content:
## Composition with consumer-side `[secrets.*]`

Both secret layers can be used together freely:

| Layer | Where declared | Where extracted | Where injected |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Consumer-side** | the consumer project's regular `Coastfile` | `coast build` time, into `coast_image = "<consumer_project_image>"` | inside the consumer's coast DinD (env vars in compose, file mounts via `docker exec` materialization) |
| **SSG-side** | `Coastfile.shared_service_groups` | `coast ssg build` time, into `coast_image = "ssg:<project>"` | inside the SSG's outer DinD via the per-run `compose.override.yml` |

There is no namespace overlap (`<project>` vs `ssg:<project>`), no injection-target overlap (the consumer's app containers vs the SSG's inner services), and no shared run-time state. Mental model:

- **Use consumer-side `[secrets.*]`** when your app needs a credential the SSG doesn't (a third-party API key, a JWT signing key your app validates).
- **Use SSG-side `[secrets.*]`** when a shared service needs a credential at startup (`POSTGRES_PASSWORD`, an inner service's TLS cert).

sections.heading: Why an override file instead of `docker exec`?
sections.content:
## Why an override file instead of `docker exec`?

The consumer-side `file:` inject pipeline uses `docker exec` to materialize secret bytes inside the consumer's coast DinD before compose-up. The SSG side picks a different mechanism for three reasons:

- Bind-mounting a host scratch dir at `/coast-runtime/` is much cheaper than a privileged exec for every secret.
- `-f` compose layering is a built-in feature; the inner compose stack handles the env/volume merge natively. No custom rewrite logic.
- Keeping the artifact dir (`/coast-artifact/`) read-only is a build-pipeline invariant. The override lives outside it, so build artifacts stay byte-stable.

sections.content:

sections.heading: SSG Volumes
sections.content:
# SSG Volumes

Inside `[shared_services.<name>]`, the `volumes` array uses the standard Docker Compose syntax:

```toml
[shared_services.postgres]
image = "postgres:16"
ports = [5432]
volumes = ["/var/coast-data/postgres:/var/lib/postgresql/data"]
env = { POSTGRES_USER = "coast", POSTGRES_PASSWORD = "coast" }
```

A leading `/` means a **host bind path** -- bytes live on the host filesystem and the inner service reads and writes them in place. Without a leading slash, e.g. `pg_wal:/var/lib/postgresql/wal`, the source is a **Docker named volume that lives inside the SSG's nested Docker daemon** -- it survives `coast ssg rm` and is dropped by `coast ssg rm --with-data`. Both forms are accepted.

Rejected at parse: relative paths (`./data:/...`), `..` components, container-only volumes (no source), and duplicate targets within one service.

sections.heading: Reusing a Docker volume from docker-compose or an inline shared service
sections.content:
## Reusing a Docker volume from docker-compose or an inline shared service

If you already have data sitting inside a host Docker named volume -- from `docker-compose up`, from an inline `[shared_services.postgres] volumes = ["infra_postgres_data:/..."]`, or from a hand-rolled `docker volume create` -- you can have the SSG read the same bytes by bind-mounting the volume's underlying host directory:

```toml
[shared_services.postgres]
image = "postgres:16"
ports = [5432]
volumes = [
    "/var/lib/docker/volumes/infra_postgres_data/_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data",
]
env = { POSTGRES_USER = "coast", POSTGRES_PASSWORD = "coast" }
```

The left side is the host filesystem path of an existing Docker volume; `docker volume inspect <name>` reports it as the `Mountpoint` field. Coast doesn't copy bytes -- the SSG reads and writes the same files docker-compose did. `coast ssg rm` (without `--with-data`) leaves the volume untouched, so docker-compose can keep using it too.

> **Why not just `infra_postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data`?** That works for inline `[shared_services.*]` (the volume gets created on the host Docker daemon, where docker-compose can see it). It does *not* work the same way inside an SSG -- a name without a leading slash creates a fresh volume inside the SSG's nested Docker daemon, isolated from the host. Use the volume's mountpoint path instead when you want to share data with anything that runs on the host daemon.

sections.heading: `coast ssg import-host-volume`
sections.content:
### `coast ssg import-host-volume`

`coast ssg import-host-volume` resolves the volume's `Mountpoint` via `docker volume inspect` and emits (or applies) the equivalent `volumes` line, so you don't hand-construct the `/var/lib/docker/volumes/<name>/_data` path.

Snippet mode (default) prints the TOML fragment to paste:

```bash
coast ssg import-host-volume infra_postgres_data \
    --service postgres \
    --mount /var/lib/postgresql/data
```

The output is a `[shared_services.postgres]` block with the new `volumes = [...]` entry already merged in:

```text

sections.heading: Add the following to Coastfile.shared_service_groups (infra_postgres_data -> /var/lib/postgresql/data):
sections.content:
# Add the following to Coastfile.shared_service_groups (infra_postgres_data -> /var/lib/postgresql/data):

[shared_services.postgres]
image = "postgres:16-alpine"
ports = [5432]
volumes = [
    "/var/lib/docker/volumes/infra_postgres_data/_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data",
]
env = { POSTGRES_PASSWORD = "coast" }

sections.heading: Bind line: /var/lib/docker/volumes/infra_postgres_data/_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
sections.content:
# Bind line: /var/lib/docker/volumes/infra_postgres_data/_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
```

Apply mode rewrites `Coastfile.shared_service_groups` in place and saves the original to `Coastfile.shared_service_groups.bak`:

```bash
coast ssg import-host-volume infra_postgres_data \
    --service postgres \
    --mount /var/lib/postgresql/data \
    --apply
```

Flags:

- `<VOLUME>` (positional) -- host Docker named volume. Must already exist (`docker volume inspect` is the check); create or rename with `docker volume create` first otherwise.
- `--service` -- the `[shared_services.<name>]` section to edit. The section must already exist.
- `--mount` -- absolute container path. Relative paths are rejected. Duplicate mount paths on the same service are hard-errors.
- `--file` / `--working-dir` / `--config` -- SSG Coastfile discovery, same rules as `coast ssg build`.
- `--apply` -- rewrite the Coastfile in place. Cannot be combined with `--config` (inline text has nothing to write back to).

The `.bak` file contains the original bytes verbatim, so you can recover the exact pre-apply state.

`/var/lib/docker/volumes/<name>/_data` is the path Docker has used as a volume mountpoint for many years and is what `docker volume inspect` reports today. Docker doesn't formally promise to keep this path forever; if a future Docker release moves volumes elsewhere, re-run `coast ssg import-host-volume` to pick up the new path.

sections.heading: Permissions
sections.content:
## Permissions

Several images refuse to start when their data directory is owned by the wrong user. Postgres (UID 999 in the debian tag, UID 70 in the alpine tag), MySQL/MariaDB (UID 999), and MongoDB (UID 999) are the common offenders. If the host directory is owned by root, Postgres exits at startup with a terse "data directory has wrong ownership".

The fix is one command:

```bash

sections.heading: postgres:16 (debian)
sections.content:
# postgres:16 (debian)
sudo chown -R 999:999 /var/coast-data/postgres

sections.heading: postgres:16-alpine
sections.content:
# postgres:16-alpine
sudo chown -R 70:70 /var/coast-data/postgres
```

Run this before `coast ssg run`. If the directory does not exist yet, `coast ssg run` creates it with default ownership (root on Linux, your user on macOS through Docker Desktop). That default is usually wrong for Postgres. If you came in via `coast ssg import-host-volume` and `docker-compose up` had previously chown'd the volume on first start, you're already fine.

sections.heading: `coast ssg doctor`
sections.content:
## `coast ssg doctor`

`coast ssg doctor` is a read-only check that runs against the current project's SSG (resolved from the cwd `Coastfile`'s `[coast].name` or `--working-dir`). It prints one finding per `(service, host-bind)` pair in the active build, plus secret-extraction findings (see [Secrets](SECRETS.md)).

For each known image (Postgres, MySQL, MariaDB, MongoDB) it consults a built-in UID/GID table, compares against `stat(2)` on each host path, and emits:

- `ok` when the owner matches the image's expectation.
- `warn` when it diverges. The message includes the `chown` command to fix it.
- `info` when the directory does not exist yet, or when the matching image has only named volumes (nothing to check from the host side).

Services whose images aren't in the known-image table are silently skipped. Forks like `ghcr.io/baosystems/postgis` are not flagged -- the doctor would rather say nothing than emit a wrong warning.

```bash
coast ssg doctor
```

Sample output with a mismatched Postgres directory:

```text
SSG 'b455787d95cfdeb_20260420061903' (project cg): 1 warning(s), 0 ok, 0 info. Fix the warnings before `coast ssg run`.

  LEVEL   SERVICE              PATH                                     MESSAGE
  warn    postgres             /var/coast-data/postgres                 Owner 0:0 but postgres expects 999:999. Run `sudo chown -R 999:999 /var/coast-data/postgres` before `coast ssg run`.
```

Doctor doesn't modify anything. Permissions on bytes you put on your host filesystem aren't something Coast silently mutates.

sections.heading: Platform notes
sections.content:
## Platform notes

- **macOS Docker Desktop.** Raw host paths must be listed under Settings -> Resources -> File Sharing. Defaults include `/Users`, `/Volumes`, `/private`, `/tmp`. `/var/coast-data` is **not** in the default list on macOS -- prefer `$HOME/coast-data/...` for fresh paths, or add `/var/coast-data` to File Sharing. The `/var/lib/docker/volumes/<name>/_data` form is *not* a host path -- Docker resolves it inside its own VM -- so it works without a File Sharing entry.
- **WSL2.** Prefer WSL-native paths (`~`, `/mnt/wsl/...`). `/mnt/c/...` works but is slow because of the 9P protocol bridging the Windows host filesystem.
- **Linux.** No gotchas.

sections.heading: Lifecycle
sections.content:
## Lifecycle

- `coast ssg rm` -- removes the SSG's outer DinD container. **Volume contents are untouched**, host bind-mount contents are untouched, keystore is untouched. Anything else that uses the same Docker volume keeps working.
- `coast ssg rm --with-data` -- drops volumes that live **inside the SSG's nested Docker daemon** (the `name:path` form without a leading slash). Host bind mounts and external Docker volumes are still untouched -- Coast doesn't own them.
- `coast ssg build` -- never touches volumes. Only writes a manifest and (when `[secrets]` is declared) keystore rows.
- `coast ssg run` / `start` / `restart` -- creates host bind-mount directories if they don't exist (with default ownership -- see [Permissions](#permissions)).
